title: Unplucked Gems: The Tragically Hip Sessions Tracker
description: A comprehensive catalogue of every known unreleased recording, demo, outtake, and alternate version from The Tragically Hip.

sessions.name: Another Midnight [V1]
sessions.notes: This version appears on the 1986 Ken Greer demo and includes entirely different lyrics not heard in any other known recording, though the sound quality is somewhat lacking.

sessions.name: Cemetery Sideroad [V1]
sessions.notes: This version of “Cemetery Sideroad” comes from a 1986 demo, though it is unclear how it differs from the album release. It may be a rough mix or even a finished version — ultimately, little is known about the tape it originates from.

sessions.name: Dreams In A Basket
sessions.notes: This song was written by Gord Downie and Gord Sinclair, and was recorded on a 4-track in 1987. The tape that this song was contained on was labeled as “The Melancholy Hip.” and was gifted to their manager at the time Allan Gregg. The song is an acoustic ballad with absolutely no drums has background vocals from Gord Sinclair

sessions.notes: Originally recorded during the Ken Greer demo sessions, this track went unreleased until the 1988 demo surfaced on the Up To Here Box Set. This version is a noteably faster than the released demo.

sessions.name: She's Got What It Takes [Greer Demo]
sessions.notes: Originally recorded during the Ken Greer demo sessions, this track went unreleased until a more refined version surfaced on the Up To Here Box Set. The earlier take features a distinctively twangier sound and opens with a guitar-only intro that doesn't appear in the later release. Notably, the song was performed live just once—on July 15, 1988—making it a rare piece in the band's history.

sessions.name: C# Jam [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This mostly instrumental jam features an intro reminiscent of "Wheat Kings," despite the earliest known demo of "Wheat Kings" predating it by about two months. It was recorded during the February 1992 demo sessions, and it is unknown whether any additional versions exist.

sessions.name: Fifty-Mission Cap [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Fifty-Mission Cap features rougher vocals with a noticeably different delivery from the released track, along with a few minor lyrical variations. The guitar tone also differs, featuring a slightly altered picking pattern compared to the final version.

sessions.name: Fifty-Mission Cap [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Fifty-Mission Cap is much more developed than the demo recorded a month earlier, using nearly the same vocal delivery as the final version. Paul’s background vocals are still being refined, and the final outro is not yet included.

sessions.name: Fifty-Mission Cap [Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Fifty-Mission Cap features a crunchier guitar tone compared to the released track. It surfaced with the rest of the Fully Completely demo disc, and although its exact recording date is unknown, it likely came after the demos listed above.

sessions.name: Fully Completely [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of "Fully Completely" is very close to the released version musicaly. The only notable differences are some differences in the vocal delivery.

sessions.name: Fully Completely [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Fully Completely includes an uncut drum count-in not heard on any other known version. It also features a slightly faster vocal delivery and minor lyrical variations.

sessions.name: Funniest Guy [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of "Funniest Guy" is very different to the leaked version notably being a bit slower and the bass guitar is a lot more prominent in this version.

sessions.name: Blond Solid [Demo]
sessions.notes: This version has a more tight arrangement compared to the one on the December 1991 Demo. This leaked along with the rest of the Fully Completely Demo disc. It's currently unknown when this is from.

sessions.name: Dumpin' The Body [December 1991 Demo]
altNames: Locked In The Trunk Of A Car
sessions.notes: This is the earliest take of "Locked In The Trunk Of A Car" that is in circulation.  It has minor lyrical differences as well as different solo.

sessions.name: Dumpin' The Body [January 8th 1992 Demo]
altNames: Locked In The Trunk Of A Car
sessions.notes: This version of "Locked In The Trunk Of A Car" is a later take than the one featured on the December 1991 Demo, with a few lyrical differences compared to the released version.

sessions.name: Eldorado [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This track was recorded during the February 1992 demo sessions. The drums are noticeably quieter, and the riff is played with slight variations compared to later versions.

sessions.name: Lionized (Take 1) [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This aborted first take of "Lionized" is a lot slower compared to the released version.

sessions.name: Lionized (Take 2) [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This second version of "Lionized", recorded in February 1992, is a full take and plays at a much slower tempo than the released version.

sessions.name: Looking For A Place To Happen [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of "Looking Of A Place To Happen" features slightly more simplified lyrics and a rougher transition to the solo.
sessions.displayName: Looking For A Place To Happen

sessions.name: Martin Sheen [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: Martin Sheen is a song that was recorded during the Fully Completley demo sessions. This one has a very catchy chorus this song kinda has a sound reminicent of The Wherewithal but slower.

sessions.name: Me In The Everglades [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This song was recorded during the February 1992 demo sessions. The full lyrics are available on LyricsOnDemand under the Eldorado Lyrics. It’s unclear whether any additional demos of this track exist.

sessions.name: Montreal [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Montreal is a later take than the one featured on Saskadelphia, with a few subtle differences compared to the earlier recording.

sessions.name: Ouch [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Ouch is a later take than the one featured on Saskadelphia, with a few subtle differences compared to the earlier recording such as extra guitar parts added.

sessions.name: Pigeon Camera [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This song was recorded during the February 1992 demo sessions. This version is very rough compared to the other demo in circulation. Differences include an unresolved outro and some different vocal delivery.

sessions.name: Pigeon Camera [Fast Demo]
sessions.notes: This song was most likely recorded after the demo above. This version is a lot more put together compared to the other demo in circulation. Differences include different drums and a faster BPM.

sessions.name: Radio Show [Early Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Radio Show is likely the earliest we have, as it sounds the roughest compared to all other recordings. However, there is currently no information available about when it was recorded.

sessions.name: Radio Show [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Radio Show is slightly more developed than the earlier one currently available to the public.

sessions.name: Radio Show [Durmless Version]
sessions.notes: This version was allegedly uploaded to the band’s website in March 2006, though no evidence of that has surfaced. Some have claimed it originates from 1995, but that seems highly doubtful. It was most likely recorded before the version released on the Fully Completely deluxe edition, as the structure is nearly identical—the key differences being the absence of drums and a more laid-back delivery with a clean guitar tone.

sessions.name: Radio Show [Deluxe Version]
sessions.notes: Released in 2014 as part of the Fully Completely deluxe edition campaign, Radio Show appeared as a promotional single alongside an early version of So Hard Done By. It remains unclear whether the band added any overdubs for this release or if it represents an untouched 1992 recording.

sessions.name: So Hard Done By [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of So Hard Done By is a different take from the one included on the Scared single and the Fully Completely deluxe edition, featuring a few subtle differences.

sessions.name: So Hard Done By [Fast Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of So Hard Done By, included on the Scared single and the Fully Completely deluxe edition, is considerably faster than the version later released on Day for Night. It likely originates from the Fully Completely sessions, as it closely resembles the December 1991 demo.

sessions.name: Wavering [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This is the earliest take of the unreleased song "Unwavering" that is in circulation. This take has a more of a Road Apples esque tone to it.

sessions.name: Unwavering [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of "Unwavering" is a bit faster than the prior version. It also contains some additional guitar licks.

sessions.name: Weather Wise [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This "Fully Completely" outtake has a very heavy "Road Apples" sound sort of reminiscing a faster version of "Bring It All Back"

sessions.name: Weather Wise [January 8th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This January version of Weather Wise is much more fully developed and carries more of a Fully Completely–style tone.

sessions.name: We'll Go Too [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of "We'll Go Too" has a couple lyrical changes compared to the released version. Besides that not much else is different.

sessions.name: Wheat Kings [December 1991 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Wheat Kings features an electric arrangement with a Road Apples-style tone and includes drums, giving it a more full-band feel. Some might argue it’s even stronger than the officially released version.

sessions.name: Wheat Kings [January 20th 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: This Is Our Life shows a tape containing a different take of “Wheat Kings,” but its specific differences remain undisclosed.

sessions.name: You See Details [February 1992 Demo]
sessions.notes: The title later became a line in Thugs, though the songs share no other similarities. Lyrically, it’s actually closer to An Inch an Hour. It was performed live only once on 4/18/92, with a few lines resurfacing in jams during 1995–96. The song was eventually reworked into the Phantom Power outtake Bumblebee.

sessions.name: You See Details [Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of You See Details features a crunchier guitar tone compared to the one above. It surfaced alongside the rest of the Fully Completely demo disc, but its exact recording date remains unknown.

sessions.name: Same Way [October/December 1993 Demo]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: At least one demo likely exists from the October–December 1993 period, as Gord Downie stated on page 126 of This Is Our Life that the song was worked on during this time.

sessions.name: Same Way [January 11th 1994 Demo]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: During the Woolen Mill sessions on January 11, 1994, a version of “An Inch An Hour” was recorded under the working title “Same Way.” The tape containing this recording is shown on page 127 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Makes Me Feel The Same Way [January 12th 1994 Demo]
sessions.notes: A version of “An Inch An Hour” was recorded on January 12, 1994, during the Woolen Mill sessions under the title “Makes Me Feel the Same Way.” The tape containing this recording is shown on page 127 of This Is Our Life.
sessions.displayName: Makes Me Feel The Same Way

sessions.name: Inch An Hour [January 24th 1994 Take 1]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of “An Inch An Hour” was recorded on January 24, 1994, along with another take of the song. The tape containing these recordings appears on page 127 of This Is Our Life. The differences between the takes are unknown.

sessions.name: Inch An Hour [January 24th 1994 Take 2]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: On January 24, 1994, two takes of “An Inch An Hour” were recorded during the Woolen Mill sessions. The tape containing these recordings is shown on page 127 of This Is Our Life, though the differences between the takes remain unknown.

sessions.name: Inch [April 1994 Version]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of “An Inch an Hour,” simply referred to as “Inch,” appears on a track list from April 1994. It is currently unknown how much this version differs from others. The list itself is documented on page 130 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Inch An Hour [Rough Mix]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of “An Inch an Hour” appears on a tape labeled Rough Mixes from May 1994. The most notable difference in this mix is the presence of a synth buried within the track. The tape itself is documented on page 127 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: An Inch An Hour [May 6th 1994 Version]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of “An Inch an Hour” appears on a list of tracks reviewed on May 6, 1994. A note beside it reads “new vocal – vamp,” likely indicating that a different vocal take was used for the final album release compared to this version. The track list is documented on page 131 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Inch An Hour [Comp #5 May 7th 1994]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of “An Inch an Hour” is contained on a tape labeled Comp #5  May 7, 1994. It is unclear how different this version is from the one reviewed on May 6, though the short time between the two suggests there were likely minimal changes. The tape is documented on page 127 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Inch An Hour [Terrarium Version]
altNames: Makes Me Feel The Same Way
sessions.notes: This version of An inch An Hour is contained on a tape titled Terrarium but unfortunatly theres no date on the but this is likely after the rest of the tapes contained on page 127 of This Is Our Life due to the tape having a rough title and a close tracklist to the final album.

sessions.name: Cowboy Coffee [V1]
sessions.notes: There may be a version of this song from the Day for Night sessions, as it appears on a list of previously unreleased tracks under that section. This list is documented on page 154 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Daredevil [October 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Gord Downie mentioned on page 126 of "This Is Our Life" that “Daredevil” was worked on between October and December 1993. It is unknown how this version differs from the two versions that followed.

sessions.name: Daredevil [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Daredevil is noticeably slower than the released version and lacks the drum intro found in later versions of the song.

sessions.name: Daredevil [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This is the slowest Daredevil recording known of and is slightly more developed than the previous version.

sessions.name: Daredevil [Rough Mix #1]
sessions.notes: It is unknown whether this mix of the song includes the section that was clipped from the intro of the released version; beyond that, no additional information about this mix is available.

sessions.name: Daredevil [Rough Mix #2]
sessions.notes: There are two tracks are listed as ‘Daredevil’ on the May 1994 Rough Mixes cassette, though it is unknown whether they differ from each other. Beyond that, no additional information about this mix is available.

sessions.name: Emergency [October 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Gord Downie noted on page 126 of "This Is Our Life" that “Emergency” was worked on between October and December 1993, making it likely that a version from sometime in October exists, though this remains unconfirmed.

sessions.name: Emergency Without End [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Emergency is noticeably slower and less heavy than the released version, and it omits the intro.

sessions.name: Endless Emergency [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Emergency is a lot closer to the released version being a lot faster than the prior demo.

sessions.name: Emergency [30-Hour Famine Version]
sessions.notes: This version of "Emergency," released on the 30 Hour Famine benefit compilation album, closely resembles the December 1993 demo but features added background vocals by Sarah McLachlan.

sessions.name: Emergency [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Emergency, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Pointy Teeth [Roadside Attraction Version]
sessions.notes: Mentioned on page 126 of "This Is Our Life", the song was being developed during the Another Roadside Attraction tour and was referred to at the time as “Pointy Teeth.” It is highly likely that an early demo exists, though this remains unconfirmed.

sessions.name: Fire In The Hole [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Fire In The Hole, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Grace Too [October 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: It is highly likely that an earlier recording of “Grace Too” exists, predating the demo listed below, as Gord Downie notes on page 126 of *This Is Our Life* that the band worked on the song from October to December 1993 along with many other developing ideas.

sessions.name: Grace [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version features a few minor musical differences, including a slightly slower tempo and no Paul vocals.

sessions.name: Grace Too [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Gord delivers a slightly softer vocal performance than on the previous demo, while this version also features Paul’s background vocals and punchier drums.

sessions.name: Grace [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Grace Too, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Greasy Jungle [October/December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Page 126 of *This Is Our Life* notes that “Greasy Jungle” was developed during the Woolen Mill sessions between October and December 1993.

sessions.name: Greasy Jungle [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Greasy Jungle, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Heavy When He Runs [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Recorded during the November 1993 sessions, this outtake exudes a heavy Road Apples-era energy.

sessions.name: Impossibilium [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Impossibilium is significantly slower than the Day for Night release and does not include the solo.

sessions.name: Impossibilium [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Impossibilium, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Inevitability [January 11th 1994 Demo]
sessions.notes: The first version of “Inevitability of Death” was recorded in January 1994, as stated by Gord Downie on page 126 of "This Is Our Life". It is unclear how different this version is from the album release.

sessions.name: Inevitability [January 12th 1994 Demo]
sessions.notes: The first version of “Inevitability of Death” was recorded in January 1994, as stated by Gord Downie on page 126 of "This Is Our Life". It is unclear how different this version is from the album release.

sessions.name: Inevitability [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Inevitability Of Death, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Laminar Flow
sessions.notes: It is referenced on page 122 of This Is Our Life by Rob, who notes that the song was never actually recorded, though it was developed during the Day For Night sessions. Despite this, it was performed multiple times on the Another Roadside Attraction Tour.

sessions.name: Limelight (Rush Cover)
sessions.notes:
A Rush cover song was recorded and presented to the members of Rush in honor of their 1994 induction into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame. A 28-second excerpt was shared during the ceremony. Dana later found this response to a bootleg request in an old issue of Tales From The Hip:
“The only complete copy was given to our friends in the mighty Rush. So unless you can give them a call and ask to borrow it, it’s not available.”

sessions.name: Light House [June/July 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: It is mentioned on page 126 of This Is Our Life, alongside the Summer 1993 version of “Scared.” It is likely that more than one demo of “Nautical Disaster” exists from their “Sleep-In” sessions.

sessions.name: Light House [July/September 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Mentioned on page 126 of *This Is Our Life*, this song was essentially fully developed during the Another Roadside Attraction tour, where it was performed multiple times as part of the mid-section of “New Orleans Is Sinking.”

sessions.name: Nautical Disaster [Acoustic Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of “Nautical Disaster” was heard briefly in Episode 2 of the This Is Our Life mini-series. Unlike the later version that appeared on Day for Night, this take features a stripped-down acoustic arrangement. It was likely recorded during one of the band’s late-night acoustic jams, which they reportedly held due to studio curfews being in place at the time. This situation is similar to the acoustic version of “Little Bones” that eventually appeared on the bonus disc of the Road Apples (Deluxe Edition).

sessions.name: Nautical Disaster [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of Nautical Disaster is the slowest known, running approximately five minutes. It also features a brief false start, with the guitar entering earlier than in the other demo.

sessions.name: Nautical Disaster [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This faster version of Nautical Disaster, recorded in December 1993, features a noticeably more upbeat tempo. The intro notably omits the guitar found on the released version, and overall it has less punch compared to the final release.

sessions.name: Nautical Disaster [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Nautical Disaster, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Overthrow [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This track is an outtake from the December 1993 sessions and was included on the Day for Night Demos disc. It was originally believed to be titled "Dante," but Darius later confirmed that the correct title is "Overthrow," as indicated on the demo tape J-card.

sessions.name: Scared [Summer 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Page 126 of "This Is Our Life" describes it as a "happy melody" Paul was developing during the Sleep-In sessions in the summer of 1993, and it is highly likely that more than one demo from these sessions exists.

sessions.name: Make You Scared [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Scared, featuring an all-electric arrangement and rougher vocals compared to later recordings.

sessions.name: Make You Scared [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This demo of "Scared" is from the December 1993 sessions and was included on the Day for Night Demos disc. This version is a lot more raw containing some electric guitar and a bit of a highter tempo.

sessions.name: Scared [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Scared, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Sherpa [Vancouver 1993 Rehearsal]
sessions.notes: This recording is from David Bastedo's "Song of the Day" collection and is labeled as the Vancouver 1993 Rehearsal. If that's accurate, it could easily be the earliest glimpse into the song..

sessions.name: So Hard Done By [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of So Hard Done By, made in May 1994.

sessions.name: Swamp Jam
sessions.notes: Gord Downie described this as "a one-off jam recorded by Mark Howard at Kingsway in New Orleans during the Day for Night sessions" when it was featured on the band’s website in 2005. Originally an instrumental, the track found new life more than a decade later as the backing music for Downie’s spoken-word piece, We Are The Next Us. Unfortunately, the original 1994 instrumental version has not been preserved or made publicly available.

sessions.name: Thugs [Fast Demo]
sessions.notes: A rough, fast rendition of “Thugs” was performed live in early 1993. It is also mentioned on page 126 of *This Is Our Life*, where it is described as a “near complete song,” though significantly different from the version that would later appear on *Day for Night*. A studio recording may exist, but this has not been confirmed.

sessions.name: Thugs [October 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: There is likely an intermediate version of “Thugs” between the Another Roadside Attraction tour rendition and the version recorded in November 1993, as Gord Downie noted on page 126 of *This Is Our Life* that the song was worked on from October to December 1993. There may even be multiple versions from this period, though this remains unconfirmed.

sessions.name: Thugs [Undated Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Thugs” appears on the back binding of the book This Is Our Life. The tape is undated, but it is likely from around the same time period, as other early versions of songs are listed under different names at the time.

sessions.name: Thugs [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version is noticeably slower than the live renditions performed throughout 1993 but retains a similar intro arrangement. Unlike the live version, which featured only guitar, this recording adds drums over the opening guitar.

sessions.name: Thugs [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version adds bongo-style drums to the intro and is slightly slower than the released version. The intro is closer to the released arrangement, consisting only of drums, though it still lacks the prominent electric guitar.

sessions.name: Thugs [Late December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version of “Thugs” is referenced by Gord Downie on page 126 of *This Is Our Life* and was recorded sometime between the week of Christmas 1993 and New Year’s Day 1994. It is said to be very close in sound to the album version.

sessions.name: Thugs [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: This version of Thugs can be seen on page

sessions.name: Titanic [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This is a completely electric rendition of Titanic Terrium, with a slightly faster tempo than the released version.

sessions.name: Titanic [December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version is very close to the released version but has a rougher mix with some alternate lyrics.

sessions.name: Titanic [Late December 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: This take of “Titanic Terrarium” would eventually become the version that appeared on *Day for Night*. Page 126 of *This Is Our Life* notes that additional takes were recorded during the main New Orleans sessions, but none captured the wintery atmosphere of this take. It is likely that there are some mixing differences as well.

sessions.name: Titanic [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: Rough mix of Titanic Terrarium from May 1994.

sessions.name: Triple D [April 1994 Version 1]
sessions.notes: Instrumental version

sessions.name: Triple D [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: This song appears on the May 1994 Rough Mixes cassette, indicating it was recorded later in the album’s development cycle. Almost nothing is known about the track, and it remains unclear how it differs from the other version also included on the same tape.

sessions.name: Triple D (with Blade) [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: This track is an especially unusual case. It shares its title and a closely similar instrumental with several other versions of “Daredevil (Reprise),” though it features completely different lyrics. The song was originally titled “Triple D,” and it remains uncertain whether any of the other tracks bearing that early name are the same recording. Commonly referred to by fans as “Hiccups,” this version is a full studio recording of the song that was also performed live by the band, with lyrics that closely resemble those heard in live performances. The track appears on the Rough Mixes May 1994 tape, as documented on page 127 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Daredevil Reprise [Scared Single Version]
sessions.notes: This track is an outtake from from the Day For Night sessions was included on the promotional single for Scared.

sessions.name: Win Win Situantion [November 1993 Demo]
sessions.notes: Introduced in 1994, and often used as an ending to Pigeon Camera. Confirmed to be recorded during the November 1993 demo.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [January 1994 Demo]
sessions.notes: The first version of “Yawning or Snarling” was recorded in January 1994, as noted by Gord Downie on page 126 of *This Is Our Life*. It is unclear how different this version is from the final release.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [January 24th 1994 Take 1]
sessions.notes: This is One of Four takes of Yawning Or Snarling recorded on

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [April 1994 Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Yawning or Snarling” appears on a list of songs from a Sunday in April, though the exact date isn’t known. The mix is described as “New 2nd section flat not spooky.” The list is shown on page 130 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [May 6th 1994 Version 1]
sessions.notes: A list of song mixes dated May 6, 1994 includes a track called “Yawning or Snarling,” with this particular mix marked by a checkmark, as shown on page 131 of This Is Our Life. However, it’s not clear how this version differs from or relates to other mixes created around that period.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [May 6th 1994 Version 2]
sessions.notes: There is a list of song mixes listened to on May 6, 1994. One of the entries is for a track titled “Yawning or Snarling,” with the second mix labeled “More Up Front [Good Mix],” as shown on page 131 of This Is Our Life. It remains unclear how much this version differs from other mixes from the same period.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [Comp #5 May 7th 1994]
sessions.notes: This version of “Yawning or Snarling” appears on a tape labeled Comp #5 – May 7th, 1994, which is shown on page 127 of This Is Our Life. It’s unclear how much this version differs from others, though it’s likely that any changes are limited to mixing differences.

sessions.name: Yawning & Snarling [Rough Mix]
sessions.notes: This rough mix of “Yawning or Snarling” was created sometime in May 1994 and appears on a tape of rough mixes titled For: The Commissioner “Hockeychrist”, which can be seen on page 127 of This Is Our Life, as well as on a rough mix tape housed in the Queen’s University Archives. It’s unclear how this version differs from the officially released one.

sessions.name: Yawning Or Snarling [Terrarium Version]
sessions.notes: There’s a version of “Yawning or Snarling” mentioned on page 127 of This Is Our Life, found on a tape labeled Terrarium. The tape isn’t dated, but it’s believed to contain either the final mixes or a later-stage version of the track from toward the end of the album’s production cycle.

sessions.name: [Unknown] Demos
sessions.notes: Demo versions likely exist for songs that were not included in the November & December 1993 demos, such as Greasy Jungle, Fire In The Hole, Yawning Or Snarling, An Inch An Hour & Inevitability Of Death. It’s also highly probable that earlier demos exist for some of the tracks that did appear on these tapes.

sessions.name: 700 Ft. Ceiling [January 1996 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version has a slower BPM compared to the released version and is about a min longer than the released version.

sessions.name: Feaverish Dream Jam [January 10th 1995 Version]
sessions.notes: This jam later developed into “Ahead by a Century” and appears on the “New Stuff #1” tape, which can be seen on page 145 of This Is Our Life. It is unclear how this early version compares to the finished track, though it is likely to differ significantly.

sessions.name: Ahead By A Century [January 11th 1995 Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Ahead by a Century,” recorded on January 11, 1995, is presumed to be more structured than the earlier jam version, though this remains unconfirmed. It was recorded just one day after the “Feaverish Dream” jam and appears on the tape labeled “New Stuff #1,” which can be seen on page 145 of This Is Our Life.

sessions.name: Ahead By A Century [Johnny & Rob Revamp]
sessions.notes: Johnny and Rob revamped the song making it less of a country based sound adding the main guitar riff and adding a drum machiuene mentioned by Rob & Gord Sinclair

sessions.name: Ahead By A Century [January 1996 Demo]
sessions.notes: This version has the dirty lyrics used in live versions with an instrumental very similar to the release version.

sessions.name: Antares [January 10th 1995 Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Anatres” appears on page 145 of This Is Our Life, documented on a tape labeled “New Stuff #1” dated between January 10 and January 12th, 1995. The tape includes three different versions of the song recorded across those dates. While the exact differences between these versions and the one later released on Passion to Skate are unknown, it is speculated that the first two recordings from January 10th and 11th feature vocals, as the January 12th version is marked “no vox” in brackets.

sessions.name: Antares [January 11th 1995 Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Anatres,” recorded on January 11th, 1995, appears on page 145 of This Is Our Life on a tape labeled “New Stuff #1.” It is one of three versions tracked across consecutive days. Although the exact differences between this recording and the version later released on Passion to Skate are unknown, it is believed to include vocals, as only the January 12th version is explicitly marked “no vox.”

sessions.name: Antares [January 12th 1995 Version]
sessions.notes: This version of “Anatres,” recorded on January 12th, 1995, is also documented on page 145 of This Is Our Life on the “New Stuff #1” tape. Unlike the earlier takes, this version is labeled “no vox,” indicating it is likely instrumental. While its precise differences from the version that appeared on Passion to Skate remain unclear, it stands apart from the previous recordings due to the apparent absence of vocals.

sessions.name: Antares [Passion To Skate Version]
sessions.notes: Kurt Browning, the renowned figure skater, counts The Tragically Hip among his favorite bands. The instrumental piece Antares was created especially for him during an intense nine-hour jam session in New Orleans, with Kurt in attendance. Originally intended to include lyrics, the song remained instrumental due to time constraints. Antares was featured in Browning’s performances and was later released on the album The Passion to Skate.

sessions.name: Unknown [Demos & Outtakes]
sessions.notes: Demo versions likely exist for songs that were not included in the January 1996 demo, such as Gift Shop, Springtime in Vienna, Butts Wigglin’, and Sherpa. It’s also highly probable that earlier demos exist for some of the tracks that did appear on the January 1996 demo. Additionally, it's possible that the December 1995 demo contains more than just the demo of Coconut Cream. Rough mixes of the entire album also almost certainly exist and are likely stored in The Hip’s vault. There is likely some outtakes from these sessions too.

After three days without bids, the seller added a “Buy It Now” option at $950. On the final day the cassette ultimately sold via a bid at the last second of the auction.

sessions.name: The Wreck Of The Edmund Fitzgerald
sessions.notes: As early as summer 2000, a studio-recorded instrumental version of Gordon Lightfoot's "The Wreck Of The Edmund Fitzgerald" began appearing on file sharing sites such as Napster, Kazaa and their descendants. The cover was credited to The Tragically Hip. A version of "The Wreck" does not appear on any album demos, it was never performed live in its entirety by the band until 2006, and nobody seemed to know why it was labeled a Hip song. Someone e-mailed the band in June of 2005, and they confirmed that The Tragically Hip have never recorded a version of the legendary Lightfoot number. Though they did receive another e-mail message from the band which read, "if it sounds like us, then it's us."

sessions.name: You Leave [9/11 Demo]
sessions.notes: A demo version of "Leave" was recorded shortly after Gord Downie witnessed the collapse of the Twin Towers on TV. This early take reportedly featured completely different lyrics focused on the attacks and themes of terrorism. According to Johnny Fay on page 215 of *This Is Our Life*, the version was likely shelved because the lyrics felt too personal.

sessions.name: You Leave [Worm Version]
sessions.notes: On page 215 of This Is Our Life, Gord Sinclair describes a tense studio exchange with Gord Downie during a recording session for "Leave". Downie suggested changing the lyric from “Where there’s love there’s hope” to “Where there’s love there’s worms” at the last minute. Producer Hugh Padgham favored the original vocal take and asked Sinclair to handle the adjustment. The disagreement created lingering tension between the two, resulting in a months-long period where they barely spoke.

sessions.notes: This acoustic demo of "Pretend" is spliced into the "In View" music video. The full version is available on Mojopaws channel.

sessions.name: Hush
sessions.notes: We Are the Same sessions B-side offered as an exclusive track for pre-ordering the album on iTunes.

sessions.name: Skeleton Park
sessions.notes: We Are the Same sessions B-side offered as an exclusive track for Telus Mobile music users.

sessions.name: Unknown [Demos & Outtakes]
sessions.notes: Very little is known about the sessions for this album, which leaves much of that period shrouded in mystery. Given the band’s known habit of recording extensively, it's likely that a number of outtakes & alternate takes were recorded but never released.

sessions.name: Wolf's Home
sessions.notes: On all three tracklists for Man Machine Poem 2 that were included in the book This Is Our Life, the song appears each time. Two of the tracklists have a question mark written beside it, while another notes “save for solo record,” which he ultimately did, recording it for the album Introduce Yerself. It remains entirely unknown whether a version of the song featuring the full band was ever recorded.

sessions.name: Dougie Stardust [Album]
sessions.notes: This was the original planned version of what would eventually become Man Machine Poem, slated for release in March 2016. It featured entirely different artwork and marketing built around the “Dougie Stardust” concept. Following David Bowie’s death, the playful Stardust reference likely felt ill-timed, and Gord’s diagnosis soon after led to the plan being shelved. It remains unknown whether there were any differences in the tracklist or the album’s mixes.

sessions.name: Man Machine Poem 2 [Album]
sessions.notes: There was a planned companion album to Man Machine Poem that was ultimately scrapped. It would have featured some of the same songs with alternate production, along with non-album tracks. In interviews, Rob mentioned they had recorded around 20 songs in total.

sessions.name: Living Room Sessions [Unknown]
sessions.notes: Although some media outlets referred to Man Machine Poem as the band’s final album, reports suggest they continued working on new studio material after its release.
